coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal method became the main preparation method of the biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by virtue of its strong operability and eco-friendliness. However, due to the differences of carbon precursors’ composition and the lack of statistical analysis of data in the synthesis process, hydrothermal synthesis process optimization was seriously insufficient, which had become the bottleneck of high − quality preparation and application of biomass CQDs. In this paper, the response surface methodology (RSM) was applied to optimize the hydrothermal synthesis process of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs, so as to regulate the CQDs’ structure and properties by simply changing the synthesis parameters. In addition, the formation mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of the CQDs were interpreted by Materials Studio (MS) and density functional theory (DFT); and the morphology, FTIR spectrum, XPS spectrum, Zeta potential, fluorescence spectrum, UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence lifetime of the target CQDs were used to verify the interpretation. In coumarin parent nucleus, by introducing strong electron-donating groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups at C6&C7 and electron-withdrawing groups such as siloxy and aldehyde groups at C3&C4, the whole molecule formed a push–pull electron system, thus enhancing excitation-dependent fluorescence of CQDs. Finally, based on the good water solubility, fluorescence excitation dependence and fluorescence stability of the target CQDs, it was proved that the Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs had great application potential in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting. This study provided a new way for the high-value utilization of Lophatherum gracile.

head.ce:title: Optimization of the hydrothermal synthesis process and its formation mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal method became the main preparation method of the biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by virtue of its strong operability and eco-friendliness. However, due to the differences of carbon precursors’ composition and the lack of statistical analysis of data in the synthesis process, hydrothermal synthesis process optimization was seriously insufficient, which had become the bottleneck of high − quality preparation and application of biomass CQDs. In this paper, the response surface methodology (RSM) was applied to optimize the hydrothermal synthesis process of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs, so as to regulate the CQDs’ structure and properties by simply changing the synthesis parameters. In addition, the formation mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of the CQDs were interpreted by Materials Studio (MS) and density functional theory (DFT); and the morphology, FTIR spectrum, XPS spectrum, Zeta potential, fluorescence spectrum, UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence lifetime of the target CQDs were used to verify the interpretation. In coumarin parent nucleus, by introducing strong electron-donating groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups at C6&C7 and electron-withdrawing groups such as siloxy and aldehyde groups at C3&C4, the whole molecule formed a push–pull electron system, thus enhancing excitation-dependent fluorescence of CQDs. Finally, based on the good water solubility, fluorescence excitation dependence and fluorescence stability of the target CQDs, it was proved that the Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs had great application potential in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting. This study provided a new way for the high-value utilization of Lophatherum gracile.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with particle size less than 10 nm [1]. It not only has light luminescence, ultraviolet absorption, high chemical stability and good electrical conductivity similar to semiconductor quantum dots, but also has advantages that semiconductor quantum dots does not have, such as good water solubility, low synthesis cost, good biocompatibility, easy surface functionalization modification, photobleaching resistance [1–3]. Besides, its particle size and its distribution, surface morphology, chemical composition, internal carbon skeleton structure and surface chemical groups can be regulated by simply changing synthesis parameters [4,5], so CQDs may become a potential substitute for traditional semiconductor quantum dots in the fields of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting [6]. It has been proved that heteroatom self-doping enriches the surface groups of CQDs, leading to a higher fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence intensity [7]. As the most abundant carbon material in nature, biomass not only contains abundant heteroatoms, but also has the advantages of a wide range of sources, environmental friendliness and low cost [8,9]. When using biomass as the carbon precursor of CQDs, self-doping of heteroatoms can be easily realized in the synthesis of CQDs without introducing additional heteroatom dopants or complex passivation processes [8]. Therefore, biomass-based CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties has become a research hotspot in the field of biomass nanomaterials [10].

As a typical biomass, Lophatherum gracile is homologous to medicine and food, containing rich coumarin glycosides, flavonoids, polysaccharides, phenolic acids and other bioactive substances [11,12]. Coumarin compounds are one of the most widely used fluorophores because of their small molecular weight [13], easy synthesis and modification, and flexible design as multi-functional and multi-type fluorescent derivatives [14,15]. However, the fluorescence of coumarin parent nucleus itself is weak, and the photophysical properties of its derivatives strongly depend on the functional groups directly bound to them, so it is necessary to modify the structure of the coumarin parent nucleus [16]. By introducing heteroatoms or electron-donor groups into C6 & C7, and electron-withdrawing groups into C3 & C4, the effective intramolecular charge transfer process (ICT) can be created in derivatives, and high fluorescence quantum yield can be achieved to enhance the optical properties of coumarin parent nuclear fluorophore [13]. The obtained coumarin derivatives have considerable application prospects in the fields of cell imaging, biomarkers and living cell sensors due to their characteristics of high photoluminescent quantum yield, good biocompatibility and water solubility, as well as strong fluorescence emission [17]. Therefore, theoretically, Lophatherum gracile are an ideal carbon precursor for fluorescent CQDs. However, at the present, only a small number of leaves have been effectively utilized, while many roots and stems are regarded as waste stage [18]. How to realize the high value-added and multi-functional utilization of Lophatherum gracile is a technical bottleneck. Considering that in recent years, some biomass carbon sources, such as Chinese herbal residues [7], turtle shell [19], and lignin [20], etc., have been successfully used for the synthesis of heteroatom doped CQDs and have shown good application potential in the fields of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting by virtue of their good biocompatibility and fluorescence stability as well as low cytotoxicity, this study focuses on the synthesis process of CQDs from Lophatherum gracile, and explores its application potential in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting.

Hydrothermal method is widely used in the synthesis of biomass CQDs. As the most traditional bottom-up synthesis method of CQDs, hydrothermal method has strong operability and can directly convert low-cost natural carbon source materials into CQDs [21] by using a simple one-pot method. However, the chemical composition of biomass carbon materials is complex and individual differences are large. If a scientific synthesis process is not designed for a specific biomass carbon precursor, the resulting CQDs will have problems such as large particle size, uneven particle size distribution, and low fluorescence quantum yield [22,23]. On the other hand, the complex chemical composition biomass carbon materials also hinders the in-depth study of the synthesis mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of biomass CQDs [10,22]. So far, the exact structure, formation mechanism and fluorescence origin of biomass CQDs are still under public debate. The design, preparation and application of biomass CQDs are still relatively limited, which greatly limits its development [24,25].

How to build molecular models of biomass CQDs by reference to the computer simulation technology that has been successfully used to study the synthesis mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of the traditional semiconductor quantum dots, and how to analyze the synthesis mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of biomass CQDs by using quantum chemistry methods, are urgent problems in the field of CQDs [26,27]. In recent years, researchers have used a combination of laboratory experiments and computer simulation techniques to explore the structure and fluorescence origin of CQDs [28]. In these studies, the smaller particle size of CQDs provides a possibility for molecular simulation of CQDs and further interpretation of the relationship between the structure and fluorescence properties of CQDs [29]. The use of data science tools in combination with materials science can have a profound impact on the development of CQDs [30], and this study is a good example. During the COVID-19 pandemic, laboratories were closed. With the completed laboratory experiment data and the computer simulation technology, we easily realized the anticipated research objectives according to the research schedule. First, we used RSM to optimize the hydrothermal synthesis of Lophatherum gracile-based self-doped fluorescent CQDs and established a prediction model of fluorescence quantum yield of the CQDs. Then, the molecular structure of the CQDs based on coumarin derivatives of Lophatherum gracile was designed and simulated by computer technology, and the formation mechanism in hydrothermal process and fluorescence mechanism were explained and further verified experimentally. Finally, the application potential of the CQDs in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting was explored.
Experimental materials used in this study are presented in the Supplementary file (Table S1), and all reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from Lophatherum gracile

ce:section.ce:para: In the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs, the dosage of carbon precersor, reaction temperature and its duration are the main factors affecting the obtained CQDs’ fluorescence properties [31,32]. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs is a quantitative index reflecting its fluorescence properties. Therefore, in this study, the Box-Behnken experimental Design method in Response Surface software Design expert 13 [33] was used to design and optimize the hydrothermal synthesis of the CQDs from Lophatherum gracile. Hydrothermal reaction time (X1), hydrothermal reaction temperature (X2) and dosage of Lophatherum gracile (X3) were the factors (independent variable), and fluorescence quantum yield (Y) was the response value (dependent variable), as shown in Table 1 and Table 2 . According to the preliminary experiment, three levels for each factor were selected, namely X1 (8 h, 10 h, 12 h), X2 (180 ℃, 200 ℃, 220 ℃) and X3 (0.8 g, 1.0 g, 1.2 g). A total of 17 runs of experiments were conducted; each run was repeated 3 times, and the average fluorescence quantum yield was taken for subsequent analysis.

The prediction model of fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs is shown in Eq. (1) [34],
(1)

Here, Y represents the predicted response value, namely the quantum yield; Xi and Xj represent the independent variable; β0 represents the intercept; βi, βii and βij are linear coefficients, quadratic coefficients and interaction coefficients, respectively.

According to Table 2, 60 mL of deionized water was mixed with the desired mass of Lophatherum gracile powder. After stirring with a magnetic stirrer (DF-101S, Shanghai Yushen Instrument Co. Ltd., China) for 10 min, the mixture was transferred to a stainless-steel reaction kettle lined with teflon (100 ml) and heated at different temperatures for different duration. The reaction solution removed from the kettle was cooled to room temperature, purified by a piece of filter membrane (0.22 µm) to remove residues and large particles, and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 RPM (H-1650, Cence, China). The resulting supernatant aqueous solution was CQDs original solution. The solid CQDs was obtained by vacuum freeze drying CQDs original solution (Alpha 1–2 LDplus, BMH, China) at −60 ℃ for 3 days. Each experimental run in Table 2 was performed 3 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and dispersion degree in water of the prepared CQDs were analyzed by the images of high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEM-2100 UHR, JEOL, Japan), which was operated at 200 V using ultrathin carbon film-coated copper grids. The chemical structure of the CQDs was characterized by a X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra ADLD spectrometer, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo Electron Corp., Madison, WI, USA). In the XPS test, the binding energy was calibrated according to C1s peak at 284.6 eV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and XPS of the carbon precursor, Common lophatherum herb, were also investigated. The spectra scanning range was 500–4000 cm−1. The zeta potential of the CQDs solution was tested using a zeta analyzer (ZS90, Malvern, UK). The measurement of zeta potentials for each sample was performed 3 times at 25 ℃, and the average values were used for discussion.

The optical properties of the CQDs were evaluated by fluorescence spectrum, UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence quantum yield. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum of the original solution of CQDs was recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-2700, Nippon Corporation, Japan). Both excitation slit width and emission slit width were set to 5 nm, and the scanning speed was 300 nm/min. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs was determined by a spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900, Nippon Corporation, Japan). The scanning rate was set to 300 nm/min and the range was 200–800 nm. According to the established quantum yield (QY) calculation model [35], Formula (2) was used to calculate the prepared the QY of CQDs. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid was selected as the reference (QY = 54 %), and the excitation wavelength was 360 nm.

Here, “QY” is the quantum yield of the CQDs, “I” is the integrated area of the measured fluorescence intensity, “A” is the absorbance at 360 nm, and “η” is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “R” refers to the standard with known QY and “X” for the sample.

Fluorescence decay curves of the CQDs were measured using a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980, Edinburgh, UK) at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The obtained lifetime value was fitted well via a double-exponential function as shown in Eq. (3), and the average lifetime was calculated according to Eq. (4) [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability of CQDs is directly related to its application in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting, so we first studied the resistance of the CQDs to UV bleaching, acid and alkali environment change and salt ion interference [20]. Then, the CQDs was used as fluorescent substance to prepare fluorescent ink for drawing anti-counterfeiting patterns. By studying the color change of the drawn patterns under a fluorescent lamp and an ultraviolet lamp, the feasibility of the application of the CQDs in anti-counterfeiting was explored. Specific experimental methods are as follows.

ce:para.ce:bold: (1) Fluorescence Photostability of the Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: In the UV bleaching resistance test of the CQDs, 0.5 mg/mL B-CQDs aqueous solution was prepared with solid B-CQDs and deionized water, and sealed in a colorless transparent centrifuge tube under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp in a dark room. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs aqueous solution was detected after different durations (0 h, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, 4 h, 5 h, 6 h) according to the method described in 2.3.

In the acid − alkali environment change resistance test of the CQDs, 7 portions of solid CQDs (5 mg) were first weighed and placed in 7 beakers, and 10 mL of phosphate buffer solutions with different pH values (pH = 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10) were added respectively, and the aqueous solution of CQDs was kept at the same concentration (0.5 mg/mL). After stirring well, the fluorescence intensity of 7 portions of CQDs aqueous solution with different pH values was tested according to the method described in 2.3.

In the salt ion interference test of the CQDs, different masses of NaCl were added to the CQDs aqueous solution (0.5 mg/mL) to make the NaCl concentration reach the set value (0 mol/mL, 0.02 mol/mL, 0.04 mol/mL, 0.06 mol/mL, 0.08 mol/mL, 0.1 mol/mL). After uniform mixing, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs in different NaCl concentrations was measured according to the method described in 2.3.

ce:para.ce:bold: (2) Preparation of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink using the CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Deionized water (100 mL) and the CQDs powder (0.5 g) were mixed evenly and centrifuged (RPM, H-1650, Cence, China) at 10,000 RPM for 10 min. The supernatant was used as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink (5 mg/mL).

ce:para.ce:bold: (3) Drawing fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns with the CQDs ink

ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, design a fluorescent anti-counterfeiting pattern, and then use a brush to dip the fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink onto a piece of double loop qualitative filter paper (11 cm, General Electric Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China) to draw the designed pattern, and air dry it at room temperature.

ce:para.ce:bold: (4) Fluorescent effect of the CQDs fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns

ce:section.ce:para: The filter paper with fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns was placed under fluorescent lamp and ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) respectively, and stored at 25 °C and 65 % relative humidity for 30 days. The changes of color and fluorescence intensity of the filter paper stored for 0, 15 and 30 days were visually observed and photographed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis process optimization of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 and Table 3 show the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CQDs corresponding to the 17 runs of test designed using the Response Surface method (shown in Table 1 and Table 2) and the variance analysis results of their fitting models. With the increase of 3 factors (hydrothermal reaction time, “t”; reaction temperature, “T”; dosage of Lophatherum gracile, “m”), the QY showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing, but the corresponding change amplitude of the three factors was different, that is, “t” caused the largest change in Response Surface slope, followed by “T”, and “m” was the least. It shows that “t” factor, hydrothermal reaction time, has the most significant effect on QY. This experimental phenomenon can be explained as follows.

The fluorescence of CQDs is mainly determined by the core structure of carbonization and the fluorescent functional groups incorporated or attached to the surface of carbon nuclei [37,38]. In this study, with the extension of hydrothermal reaction time (from 8.000 h to 10.392 h) and temperature (from 180.00 ℃ to 198.49 ℃), the carbonization core structure is gradually formed, the core π conjugated domain is gradually increased, and the surface functional groups are gradually formed and covalently bonded with the core structure. In this process, the QY is gradually increased. However, with the further extension of hydrothermal reaction time (from 10.392 h to 12.000 h) and temperature (from 198.49 ℃ to 220.00 ℃), the reaction becomes gradually intense, resulting in excessive carbonization of the surface of CQDs’ surface, which decreases its QY. So, within the range of this test, hydrothermal reaction time and temperature have a threshold for CQDs’ QY, namely 10.392 h and 198.49 ℃, respectively [34,39,40]. In addition, the QY gradually increases with increasing the amount of Lophatherum gracile from 0.800 g to 1.087 g; however, when the amount of carbon precursor exceedes 1.087 g, the QY decreases. So, 1.087 g is the threshold of carbon precursor amount (the corresponding amount of deionized water is 60 mL). When the amount of Lophatherum gracile is lower than 1.087 g, the introduction of carbon precursor contributes more heteroatoms and functional groups, induces more surface fluorescence groups, and thus increases the QY [41]; however, excessive carbon precursor will hinder the formation of many high-quality CQDs, because when the amount of deionized water is certain, the reaction solution with excessive solid–liquid ratio cannot provide enough reaction sites for CQDs [42].

With QY as the response value, and with “t”, “T” and “m” as independent variables, the experimental data were analyzed by quadratic polynomial regression equation, and the fitting model of QY was obtained shown as Eq. (5),
(5) QY = −19.95 + 1.39 t + 0.18 T − 1.24 m + 0.0011 t*T + 0.0056 t*m + 0.015 T*m − 0.078 t2 − 0.00052 T2 − 0.822 m2

Variance analysis of the fitting model (5) is also shown in Table 3. “P < 0.0001” indicates that the model has a very significant fitting effect on QY [43]. The missing item is not significant (P > 0.05), showing that the model is reliable [44]. According to the fitting model of (5) and the value of primary term P in Table 3, the effects of 3 factors on QY can be ranked as “t > T > m”, demonstrating that hydrothermal reaction time and temperature play a key role in the hydrothermal process of CQDs from Lophatherum gracile. This result is consistent with the Response Surface diagram in Fig. 1. In addition, the p values corresponding to t, T, t2 and m2 are all less than 0.05, which is significant; all values of R2, Adjusted R2 and Predicted R2 are close to 1, indicating that there is a good fitting relationship between experimental data and the fitting model [41]. The signal-to-noise ratio (Adeq Precision) is 19.1009, which is greater than 4, indicating that the fitting model has high precision and small experimental error [45], and can be used to effectively predict the optimal response.

According to fitting the model (5), when hydrothermal reaction time (t), hydrothermal reaction temperature (T) and the amount of Lophatherum gracile (m) are 10.392 h, 198.49 ℃ and 1.09 g, respectively, QY is maximum. Considering the feasibility of test and the actual adjustment range of the equipment, the above 3 factors was chosen as 10.4 h, 198.5℃ and 1.09 g (60 mL deionized water), respectively, and 3 parallel verification tests were carried out to obtain the target CQDs (i.e., Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs prepared by the optimal hydrothermal synthesis process). The average value of QY was 4.34±0.13 %. The deviation between this value and the calculated theoretical QY value (4.32 %) by the fitting model (5) is only 0.46 %. It indicates that the test data fit well with the predicted response of the model (5), which proves the model is authentic and credible.

In conclusion, according to the fitting model (6), the fluorescence quantum yield can be regulated by simply changing processing parameters such as hydrothermal reaction time, temperature and the amount of Lophatherum gracile, so as to control the structure and performance of the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis mechanism of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: This ideal precursor material for CQDs is rich in coumarin glycosides, flavonoids, polysaccharides, phenolic acids, heteroatoms and other bioactive substances [8,11,12]. Referring to the component analysis methods for the precursor material of CQDs in the reported studies [9,35,36] as well as the XPS elemental analysis and characterization tests, this study found that in Lophatherum gracile, C, N, O and Si elements accounted for 65.96 %, 1.39 %, 22.67 % and 9.98 % respectively. Based on the chemical composition of Lophatherum gracile and the research results of the response surface in 3.1, the formation mechanism of the Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs was discussed, and the structure of the target CQDs (Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs prepared by the optimal hydrothermal synthesis process) was predicted with the help of molecular simulation technology and quantum chemistry calculation software (MS), and the DMol3 module was used to optimize the molecular configuration, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) [28].

In the process of hydrothermal synthesis of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs, the water vapor formed under high temperature and pressure provides a dynamic reaction medium for the heterogeneous system of deionized water/Lophatherum gracile [46]. It promotes the complex pyrolysis reaction of the effective components in Lophatherum gracile. In general, such a pyrolysis reaction includes a series of processes, “oxidation decomposition − crosslinking − carbonization“ [47].

The main fluorophores in Lophatherum gracile, coumarin glycosides, require sufficient reaction time and temperature to undergo oxidation and decomposition, and the speed and degree of the reaction are closely related to the hydrothermal reaction time and temperature. Relatively short reaction time and low temperature can trigger coumarin glycosides to initially form coumarin carbon core structure with relatively uniform size through oxidative decomposition [45]. At this stage, the conjugated π domain of the carbon core structure is still small and the surface functional groups have not been formed in large numbers, so, the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon nanoparticles is low [41]. In the first stage in Fig. 2(a), the cyclocyclic dehydration of coumarin glycosides (paramo-hydroxy-cinnamic acid) in Lophatherum gracile occurs. The C6 and C7 isopentenyl groups in furanocoumarins and furanocoumarins react with o-phenol hydroxyl groups to form furan rings, which is accompanied by the decomposition of coumarins to form the coumarin parent nucleus [48,49].

The second stage in Fig. 2(a) is the crosslinking stage in the synthesis process of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs. At this time, the main forms of N element in Lophatherum gracile are glutamine and asparagine, and the main forms of Si element are metasilicic acid and free mono silicic acid [11,50]. With the increase of reaction temperature and time, the chemical bonds of substances containing N and Si elements in Lophatherum gracile are broken, and a large number of fragments are generated [51,52]. These fragment units further modify the coumarin parent nucleus and gradually polymerize to form CQD structure with a push–pull electron system with specific electron donor groups and electron withdrawing groups [48]. The process can be described as follows. A large number of coumarin parent nuclei present in Lophatherum gracile undergo amino and hydroxyl substitution reactions at C6&C7 sites, introducing strong electron-donating groups such as amino and hydroxyl into coumarin parent nuclei [13,49,53]; at the same time, electron-withdrawing groups such as siloxy and aldehyde groups are also introduced at C3&C4 sites. The corresponding molecular structure of customized CQDs drawn by MS is predicted to be about 0.9 nm. In addition, the introduction of heteroatoms such as N and Si present in Lophatherum gracile into CQDs can significantly reduce the energy gap, expand the conjugated structure, and enhance the electron delocalization ability of the CQDs [54]. Moreover, the self-doping process also increases the number of photoexcited electrons in the emission transition channel and makes the CQDs have both electron-donating groups and electron-withdrawing groups in its molecular structure (strong electron-donating groups C6&C7 sites; electron-withdrawing groups at C3&C4 sites) [15,55]. The whole molecule forms a push–pull electron system in the electronic structure, thereby inducing intramolecular charge transfer, improving the photoluminescence performance of the coumarin parent nuclei, enhancing its fluorescence and its fluorescence quantum yield [56,57].

There is an inevitable relationship between the structure of the target CQDs and its high fluorescence quantum yield, that is, the structure of the target CQDs corresponds to its high fluorescence quantum yield. The specific reasons are as follows.

(1) The description in lines 34–41 of the Introduction (references [6–10]) “Heteroatom self-doping enriches the surface groups of CQDs, leading to a higher fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence intensity”, and “When using biomass as the carbon precursor of CQDs, self-doping of heteroatoms can be easily realized in the synthesis of CQDs without introducing additional heteroatom dopants or complex passivation processes” shows that heteroatom doped CQDs has a higher fluorescence quantum yield, and biomass materials rich in heteroatoms are suitable as carbon source materials for self-doped CQDs.

(2) The description in lines 44–52 (references [13–16]) of the Introduction “By introducing heteroatoms or electron-donor groups into C6 & C7, and electron-withdrawing groups into C3 & C4, the effective intramolecular charge transfer process (ICT) can be created in derivatives, and high fluorescence quantum yield can be achieved to enhance the optical properties of coumarin parent nuclear fluorophore” illustrates the specific structure of coumarin-derived CQDs corresponds to a higher quantum yield, and provides a reason why the specific structure of coumarin-derived CQDs leads to a higher quantum yield. In addition, the results of some other studies (references [11,12,18]) also suggest that Lophatherum gracile is an ideal biomass carbon precursor for the target CQDs, and its abundant heteroatoms create conditions for obtaining coumarin-derived CQDs with a specific structure and a high fluorescence quantum yield.

(3) According to the content in lines 287–305 about synthesis process and synthesis mechanism of CQDs in the origin manuscript (Section 3.1 and Section 3.2), and with reference to other reported research results (references [11,13,15,49,51–57]), we concluded a conclusion that “The whole molecule forms a push–pull electron system in the electronic structure, thereby inducing intramolecular charge transfer, improving the photoluminescence performance of the coumarin parent nuclei, enhancing its fluorescence and its fluorescence quantum yield”.
Based on the above contents in (1), (2) and (3), we think that the structure of target CQDs corresponds to its high fluorescence quantum yield.

In order to verify the molecular structure of the customized Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield, we compare the quantum yields of biomass CQDs in other studies in recent years with that in this study (Table S2), the structure and performance of the target CQDs were analyzed.

Fig. 2(b) shows that the target CQDs presents a monodisperse state in water with uniform particle distribution and no obvious agglomeration. 100 target CQDs were randomly selected by Nano measurer software for particle size analysis, and the average size of the target CQDs is 0.89±0.10 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(c). Specially, this particle size of the target CQDs is basically consistent with the size of the molecular structure (0.9 nm) of the predicted customized CQDs mentioned above.
Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) were used to analyze the characteristic groups and chemical elements of the target CQDs.

The absorption peak at 3331.91 cm−1 in Fig. 2(d) corresponds to the stretching vibration of -OH and N-H bonds present in the carbon precursor [58]. The absorption peaks at 2917.68 cm−1 and 2849.96 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C-H bonds on saturated carbon in organic molecules [8,59]. The regions near 1632.75 cm−1 and 1317.02 cm−1 are the stretching vibration regions of -C=C- and C-N, respectively [59]. The absorption peak at 1242.73 cm−1 corresponds to the C-O stretching vibration region [60]. In Fig. 2(e), the peaks near 2929.15 cm−1 and 1402.76 cm−1 indicate the presence of C-H and C-C bonds in the target CQDs [8], demonstrating that the basic chemical structure of small molecules in the carbon precursor is still retained in the target CQDs. The peak at 1767.17 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of C=O bond [8], while the new absorption peak at 1659.94 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of the oxygen-containing functional group C=O generated in the hydrothermal process [61]. The peaks at 1593.89 cm−1 and 1515.75 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=C bond in the aromatic ring and lactone ring [62], which indicates that the conjugated π domain of CQDs is gradually formed in the hydrothermal process, corresponding to the oxidative decomposition stage of the above synthetic customized CQDs, that is, the decomposition process of coumarin glycosides to form coumarin parent nuclei [45].

Compared with the spectrum of Lophatherum gracile, the target CQDs has a wider absorption peak near 3206.81 cm−1, which is attributed to N-H bond stretching vibration or O-H stretching vibration [8]. In the high temperature and high pressure environment of hydrothermal process, Lophatherum gracile fully reacts with deionized water to form more hydrophilic groups such as -OH and -NH2 in CQDs structure [54,63]. Of particular note are the peaks of stretching vibrations at 1320.88 cm−1, 1194.52 cm−1 and 883.31 cm−1 belonging to C-N, Si-O and Si-C, respectively, which confirms that the successful connection of substituents containing heteroatom N and heteroatom Si [61]. This experimental phenomenon corresponds to the self-doping process of nitrogen and silicon heteroatom in the coumarin parent nucleus in the cross-linking stage (i.e., in the oxidative decomposition stage).

Fig. 3 shows the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of carbon precursor and the target CQDs. In Fig. 3(a and b), the XPS full spectrum of Lophatherum gracile shows four main characteristic peaks, indicating that in Lophatherum gracile, C, N, O and Si elements accounted for 65.96 %, 1.39 %, 22.67 % and 9.98 %, respectively. By fitting the XPS full spectrum in Fig. 3(a), high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s and Si2p were obtained, as shown in Fig. 3(c – f). The C1s spectrum has peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.5 eV, which is attributed to the bond of C=C, C-C/C-N, and C=O, respectively [58]. The N1s spectrum has a peak at 400.0 eV caused by the N-H bond [8]. For the O1s spectrum, two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.8 eV are attributed to C=O and C-O, respectively. Two peaks at 103.2 eV and 104.7 eV in the Si2p spectrum are caused by the bond of Si-C and Si-O, respectively [61].

For the target CQDs, the XPS full spectrum of shows four main characteristic peaks as shown in Fig. 3(g), corresponding to the binding energy of C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (400.0 eV), O1s (532.2 eV) and Si2p (102.2 eV), respectively; the element C, N, O and Si account for 62.6 %, 2.39 %, 27.5 % and 7.51 % respectively, as shown in Fig. 3(h). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s and Si2p are displayed in Fig. 3(i – l). The three peaks in the C1s spectrum at 284.9 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.5 eV belong to the C-C/C=Si, C-C/C-N and C=O, respectively [61]. Compared with the C1s spectrum of the carbon precursor, the C-C/C=Si content in the target CQDs is significantly increased, which onfirms the successful doping of heteroatom Si in the hydrothermal synthesis process. In addition, the N1s spectrum of the target CQDs has peaks attributed to N-C and N-H at 399.9 eV and 400.8 eV, respectively [61], which confirms the significant formation of N-C bonds and the successful doping of heteroatom N during hydrothermal synthesis. The fitting peaks caused by C=O and Si-O at 531.6 eV and 532.4 eV in the O1s respectively spectrum confirm the large amount of C=O formation and the successful doping of heteroatom Si in the hydrothermal synthesis process [61]. In particular, the strong evidence for successful doping of heteroatom Si in hydrothermal synthesis is the obvious difference between the Si2p spectrum of the target CQDs and that of the carbon precursor (for the carbon precursor, the Si2p spectrum has peaks caused by Si-C and Si-O bonds at 103.2 eV and 104.7 eV respectively; for target CQDs, the peaks caused by Si-O and Si-C bonds are located at 101.8 eV and 103.1 eV, respectively).

In conclusion, the results of XPS study of the carbon precursor and the target CQDs are mutually confirmed with that of FTIR spectra in Fig. 2(d – e). The target CQDs not only retains some functional groups of the carbon precursor, such as C-H, -C=C-, -C-N-, -C-O, but also has a higher content of hydrophilic functional groups than carbon precursor. In addition, in the hydrothermal process, a large number of Si-C bonds in the carbon precursor are broken, generating a large number of chemical bonds such as C=Si, Si-O and Si-N, thus realizing the self-doping of heteroatoms N and Si contained in the carbon precursor. All these helps to improve the water solubility and fluorescence stability of the target CQDs, giving it the potential application in the field of fluorescence anti-counterfeiting [64].

With the further increase of hydrothermal reaction temperature and time, the hydrothermal synthesis process of CQDs from Lophatherum gracile enters the carbonization stage, the third stage in Fig. 2(a). The monodisperse carbon nanoparticles aggregate, attach and connect with each other to form polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) clusters, and then transform into increasingly expanded carbon networks [49]. Further increasing reaction temperature and time will cause the structure of some of the formed carbon quantum dots to be destroyed, thus reducing the fluorescence and fluorescence quantum yield QY [65]. In addition, the Zeta potential test results of the target CQDs sample shows that its surface is negatively charged (−12.17 mV), which also confirms the successful doping of N and Si elements on the coumarin parent nucleus during the hydrothermal synthesis process and the presence of a large number of hydrophilic groups on the surface of CQDs. It is also consistent with the FTIR and XPS test results of the target CQDs in Fig. 2(e) and Fig. 3, and highly consistent with the prediction of functional groups in the customized CQDs’ molecular structure simulated by the quantum chemistry calculation software in this Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence mechanism of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the molecular model of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs constructed in 3.2, with the help of density functional theory (DFT), the DMol3 module of Materials Studio was used to calculate the electrostatic potential diagram the molecular structure of CQDs and the molecular orbital energy levels of frontier orbitals (including the highest occupied molecular orbital HOMO and the lowest Molecular orbital LUMO). Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) theory was also used to explain the fluorescence origin of the CQDs [66]. The detailed is as follows.

The molecular structure model of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 4 (a). Its electron donating groups (amino and hydroxyl) and electron withdrawing groups (siloxy and aldehyde) are directly connected to the fluorescent groups of coumarin parent nucleus through non-conjugated bonding bonds. The electrostatic potential diagram of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 4(b). The electron-withdrawing and electron-donating groups at positions C3 & C4, and C6 & C7 are evenly distributed in the molecular proposition, which further expands the conjugated π domain of the molecule, thus creating a more efficient intramolecular charge transfer process [13]. The calculated the energy of HOMO and LUMO is −6.23 eV and −2.14 eV, respectively, and the band gap is 4.09 eV.

The excellent fluorescence emission characteristics of the CQDs come from the push–pull effect of charge separation generated by its rigid plane structure excited by light energy. As shown in Fig. 4(c and d), the DFT calculation image reveals the distribution characteristics of excited electrons of the CQDs at HOMO and LUMO levels. The LUMO orbital is greatly stabilized by introducing electron-withdrawing groups at C3&C4 positions and electron-donating groups at C6&C7 positions of carbon core structure (coumarin parent nucleus). Compared with the result of a previous report [14] (the band gap of coumarin parent nuclear energy was 4.81 eV, the band gap of coumarin derivatives that bond the electron-withdrawing group at C3 site at coumarin parent nucleus was 4.43 eV, and the band gap decreased to 4.22 eV after further bonding an electron-donor group at para-C8 of coumarin derivatives), the target CQDs synthesized in this study has a smaller energy gap (4.09 eV). It indicates that the energy gap of CQDs can be reduced through self-doping of heteroatoms in Lophatherum gracile itself. By introducing electron-withdrawing groups into C3&C4 sites and electron-donating groups into C6&C7 sites, the LUMO orbital is greatly stabilized and the band gap between HOMO and LUMO is significantly reduced [13,67], resulting in a larger Stokes Shift (i.e., excitation dependent fluorescence emission over a larger wavelength range). This characteristic is beneficial to the application of CQDs in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting. Besides, the result (the band gap of our CQDs is smaller than those in the previous report [14]) also shows that in coumarin molecular structure, the para-substituents (heteroatomic functional groups of in C3 & C4 sites and C6 & C7sites) contribute more to molecular fluorescence than others, and the direct connection of the functional groups to the carbon core further expands the conjugated π domain, promoting the internal charge transfer of excited molecules and thus emitting fluorescence in the excited state [68,69].

To further elucidate the optical properties of the target CQDs, the UV–Visible absorption and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the target CQDs were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 4(e and f), respectively. The CQDs exhibits significant absorption in the UV wavelength range of 252–350 nm, and the maximum peak corresponds to 278 nm. It is attributed to π-π* transition of the benzene ring structure and the C=C bond in the lactone ring in the coumarin parent nucleus, and it usually indicates the successful formation of CQDs [17]. The tiny absorption peak at 246–250 nm should be the absorption band formed by n → σ* transition in heteroatoms in the substituted group directly connected to the coumarin parent nucleus [13]. In addition, the illustration in Fig. 4(e) shows the CQDs aqueous solution is light yellow under visible light and shows strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The good photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of the CQDs is due to the further modification of the coumarin parent nucleus’s molecular structure after the hydrothermal reaction [70]. The electron donor groups, the electron withdrawing groups or the heteroatoms are introduced into C6 & C7 and C3 & C4 sites, respectively, so that the whole molecular structure formed a push–pull electron system, thereby expanding the conjugated structure, enhancing the electron delocalization ability of π conjugated electron, inducing the intramolecular charge transfer and enhancing fluorescence [15,68,69]. It can be seen that the experimental results of UV–Vis absorption spectra of the target CQDs shown in Fig. 4(e) are highly consistent with the predicted results of CQDs molecular structure in Section 3.2, which proves the rationality of the prediction.

As shown in Fig. 4(f), there exists a single center similar to concentric circles in the three-dimensional fluorescence contour of the CQDs, and its optimal emission center and excitation center are located at about 398 nm and 320 nm, respectively. Such a wide fluorescence emission range can be attributed to the difference in particle size of CQDs or the difference in surface defect energy trap [71] (i.e., the multi-energy transition phenomenon caused by the multi-dispersed fluorescence centers). Besides, the experimental phenomenon that both the excitation center and the emission center of the target CQDs are located at shorter wavelengths can be explained by the test results in Fig. 2(b and c) (the target CQDs has a smaller particle size than other biomass CQDs). Based on the quantum confinement effect, as the size of CQDs decreases, its band gap increases, and the excitation and emission centers of smaller particles move toward shorter wavelengths [72]. When the target CQDs is excited by a series of monochromatic light in the strong excitation band (275–375 nm), the redshift of the maximum PL is about 30 nm, which indicates that the fluorescence of the CQDs has an excitation-related property, which is consistent with the excitation dependent phenomenon of typical coumarin derivative fluorescent materials [73,74]. Using this characteristic, the emission wavelength can be changed by adjusting the excitation wavelength, so that the CQDs can be customized according to the requirement of fluorescence anti-counterfeiting. In addition, it is found from the fluorescence emission color coordinates of CQDs in Fig. 4(g) that its CIE color coordinate is (0.1637, 0.139), which also confirms that the CQDs emits blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation.

To further explore the PL emission source of the target CQDs, its time-resolved PL attenuation curve were also analyzed, as shown in Fig. 4(h). The components τ1 and τ2 are related to the electronic transition and belong to the recombination process of nuclear state and surface state of CQDs, respectively [67,75]. When excited at 400 nm, τ1 and τ2 accounts for 24.51 % and 75.49 %, respectively, indicating that the fluorescence attenuation is mainly determined by the surface states [67,75] (self-doping heteroatoms and marginal substituent groups of coumarin parent nucleus). In summary, the particle size test result and optical properties analysis of the target CQDs show that CQDs is successfully prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method from Lophatherum gracile, and the emission wavelength can be controlled by adjusting the excitation wavelength, so that CQDs can be customized according to the requirement of fluorescence anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application potential of the CQDs in fluorescenct anti-counterfeiti
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the test results of fluorescence intensity stability of the target CQDs. In this study, the effects of UV (365 nm) irradiation duration, NaCl concentration and pH on fluorescence intensity of CQDs were studied. In Fig. 5(a – c), the horizontal coordinate is UV irradiation duration, NaCl concentration and pH value respectively, and the ordinate is the ratio of the CQDs’ fluorescence intensity (FL) to its initial fluorescence intensity (FL0) under different UV irradiation duration, NaCl concentrations and different pH environment, respectively. Here, FL0 is denoted as the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs while UV irradiation duration is 0 h or NaCl solution concentration is 0 mol/mL or pH value is 4. When the UV irradiation duration is 5 h, or the NaCl concentration is as high as 0.1 mol/L, or the pH value of the environment increases from 4 to 10, the fluorescence intensity of the target CQDs can still retain 95.03 %, 93.89 % and 92.21 %, respectively, and its fluorescence intensity is very stable in acidic and neutral environments. It indicates that the target CQDs has strong fluorescence intensity stability, which is the basic requirement of fluorescent ink for its fluorescent substance [76,77]. Based on the results of the above simulation calculation and experimental characterization of physical morphology and chemical structure of the CQDs, it can be speculated that the high fluorescence stability of the CQDs is due to the successful heteroatom doping of functional groups containing N and Si, as well as the formation of a large number of hydrophilic functional groups on the parent core of coumarin [17]. Meanwhile, the optimization synthesis process by response surface method (RSM) makes it possible to control the particle size and structure of the customized CQDs [41,78]. CQDs nanoparticles with the same or similar surface functional groups and surface defects are prone to have the same surface charge, which leads to mutual repulsion among CQDs nanoparticles. So, the CQDs presents a uniform monodisperse state colloid in water medium and has morphological stability, which not only improves the fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence intensity of CQDs, but also improves its fluorescence intensity stability [11,52].

Fig. 5(d – f) show the anti-counterfeiting application potential of the target CQDs as fluorescent ink. After storage at room temperature for different duration, the pattern drawn on ordinary filter paper using the target CQDs is colorless in daylight but shows bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. This test result confirms that the target CQDs has luminous effect only under ultraviolet irradiation, so it can be used as invisible ink to achieve and anti-counterfeiting [77]. In addition, when stored at room temperature for 1 month, the fluorescence of the target CQDs has no significant change, that is, the CQDs shows stable fluorescence for a long time under static conditions (air atmosphere). In addition, when stored at room temperature for 1 month, the fluorescence of the patterns has no significant change, showing stable fluorescence for a long time under static conditions (air atmosphere). The water-based fluorescent ink prepared with the target CQDs not only has good stability, but also has the advantages of good environmental protection, transparency and easy cleaning, so it may be a substitute for the traditional fluorescent anti-counterfeiting metal quantum dot fluorescent ink [75,79].

In the paper, RSM was used to optimize the hydrothermal synthesis process of Lophatherum gracile-derived CQDs. Its hydrothermal synthesis mechanism and fluorescence mechanism were explained by Materials Studio and DFT respectively, and verified by experiments. In addition, the application potential of the CQDs in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting was also explored.

(1) With the help of RSM, the hydrothermal synthesis process of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs was optimized. When the reaction temperature, duration and amount of carbon precursor were 198.5℃, 10.4 h and 1.09 g (60 mL DI water) respectively, the quantum yield (QY) of the target CQDs was 4.34±0.13 %, and the deviation from the QY calculated by the fitting model was only 0.46 %. The structure and properties of the CQDs could be controlled by simply changing the synthesis parameters.

(2) The molecular configuration of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs was constructed and the hydrothermal synthesis mechanism was explained using DMol3 module of MS. By introducing strong electron donor groups (amino and hydroxyl) at C6&C7, and electron withdrawing groups (siloxy and aldehyde) at C3&C4, a push–pull electron system was formed in the carbon nucleus, endorsing it with photoluminescence properties. It was verified experimentally by the particle size, FTIR and XPS spectra and Zeta potential of the target CQDs.

(3) The fluorescence origin of Lophatherum gracile-based CQDs was explained with the help of DFT and DMol3. The push–pull electron system formed during hydrothermal synthesis gave coumarin parent nuclei excellent excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, which was mainly determined by surface self-doping atoms and edge substituting groups. It was confirmed by the experimental results of the spectra of fluorescence and UV–visible, as well as fluorescence lifetime of the target CQDs.

(4) Based on the good water solubility and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of the target CQDs, the storage durability of fluorescent patterns drawn with CQDs ink proved that the CQDs had great application potential in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting.

Miaoyan Hu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Peipei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Software. Taohong Li: Writing – review & editing, Software. Kai Liu: Writing – review & editing. Hailan Lian: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition.
